q: In 1905, Albert Einstein proposed that light must also be thought of as particle or photon.
Louis de Broglie suggested that the momentum $p$ of a particle and its associated wavelength $\lambda$ are related by the equation $\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$.
The wavelength $\lambda$ associated with a moving particle (matter wave) is also called de Broglie wavelength.
All moving particles exhibit properties of both particles and waves nature.
The existence of matter waves were first experimentally confirmed by Thomson’s cathode ray diffraction experiment and the Davisson-Germer experiment for electrons.
In electron microscope, the wave nature of electrons is used instead of light.
An integral number of de Broglie wavelengths associated with a revolving electron must fit in the circumference of an orbit.
De Broglie hypothesis proves Bohr’s second postulate, which states that the angular momentum of the orbiting electron is quantized.
Wave nature can be easily observed for macroscopic particles like a basketball.
1 eV (electron volt) is the energy gained by an electron when accelerated through a potential of one volt.
